lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent operators that mine, dredge, wash, dry, screen, size, blend, and repeatedly sell industrial-grade sand to external oilfield, glass, foundry, filtration, building-products, chemical, ceramic, recreational, and other industrial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive mines serving only an affiliated manufacturer or oilfield operation; construction-grade sand and gravel pits; industrial-sand distributors or logistics providers without mining; inactive reserve holdings; shells; non-control financing vehicles; and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business product sales by ton and specification through spot, short-term, or take-or-pay contracts, often combined with rail, transload, storage, or delivery arrangements; price and volume depend on sand quality, customer qualification, proximity, logistics, end-market cycles, and competing capacity.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all NAICS 212 mining except oil and gas, not industrial sand, and NIOSH discusses automation across mining commodities. I translate the prominence of loading, equipment-operation, haulage, and supervisory work into exposure through autonomous or assisted mobile equipment, conveyor and plant control, vision, dispatch, drone surveying, predictive maintenance, lab-data review, logistics, quoting, and administration, then discount because extraction, wet and dry processing, maintenance, sampling, rail loading, and safety work remain physical; broad mining may overstate exposure if small industrial-sand plants have older equipment and variable product runs.
a.rationale: Surface mining, repeat truck or conveyor routes, screening and drying plants, laboratories, inventory, and logistics create substantial AI-enabled automation and decision-support potential. Physical repairs, process interventions, quality release, silica controls, and site accountability limit substitution, keeping the central wage-weighted exposed share at two fifths.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit OES staffing, wage-weighted task studies at frac and specialty-sand plants, and measured labor changes from autonomous haulage, process control, vision, laboratory, dispatch, and predictive-maintenance deployments would materially change the estimate.
caveats: BLS data span coal, metal, and nonmetallic mining rather than NAICS 212322.
Automation potential differs between in-basin wet frac sand, Northern White dry plants, and higher-specification industrial sands.

rho.rationale: Planning, logistics, quoting, inventory, surveying, laboratory review, and maintenance tools should be deployable within five years, while mobile autonomy and closed-loop processing require capital, connectivity, safety assurance, integration with mixed fleets, stable product flows, and trained support. A cyclical sector with idle capacity and only 44 estimated LMM firms may defer high-capital projects even when technically feasible.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year LMM industrial-sand cohort reporting AI project costs, uptime, interventions, safety events, quality deviations, labor hours, tons per employee, and payback would materially narrow the range.
caveats: NIOSH evidence establishes adoption direction and safety constraints, not a five-year implementation rate.
Demand volatility and idle facilities can weaken the business case for automation independent of technical readiness.

e.rationale: The NAICS activity is predominantly external-market mining and processing, and the USGS count of 131 companies indicates a bounded specialist sector. Eligibility is reduced for captive operations, inactive or idled plants, strategic-scale producers, logistics-only entities, and firms outside normalized EBITDA despite the frozen size bridge. The frozen estimate of 44 firms makes classification errors material.
e.change_evidence: A reconciliation of the frozen 44-firm estimate to MSHA permits and USGS operations, enriched for ownership, operating status, captive use, end-market mix, revenue, and normalized EBITDA, would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The frozen n is an estimated margin bridge rather than an observed firm roster.
USGS counted 131 companies and 207 operations in 2025 but does not identify LMM economics or captive ownership.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying transfers in NAICS 212322. I anchor near its 22% result and widen because recent industrial-sand disclosures describe consolidation, mine closures, idling, asset sales, and bankruptcies, but retain a low case because closures, reserve sales, and internal restructurings are not necessarily transferable going concerns. The general-owner proxy may overstate qualifying control sales during frac-sand oversupply.
s5.rationale: Industry restructuring and an asset-backed specialist base create acquisition opportunities, yet the small frozen universe, highly cyclical frac exposure, customer concentration, permits, reclamation, and obsolete grain-size or logistics configurations can prevent a clean transfer. A central one-in-five probability remains the most defensible anchor.
s5.change_evidence: NAICS-coded deal data from GF Data, PitchBook, CapIQ, Axial, bankruptcy records, and state permit transfers, paired with owner-age and succession interviews for the frozen firm set, would materially change the probability.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions, not completed qualifying control transfers.
Asset sales, idled-mine disposals, and bankruptcy transactions may not transfer an eligible operating firm.

q.bridge: Smart Sand is a public Northern White frac-sand and logistics supplier with a small specialty-industrial business, not an LMM industry-wide AI benefit study. Its disclosure of highly competitive markets, spot and short-term market pricing, customer reluctance to sign take-or-pay contracts, two customers contributing 39% of 2025 revenue, regional-sand substitution, and oversupply risk shows strong mechanisms for passing productivity gains to customers. I use that structure to infer below-half central retention, while the range allows higher retention for qualified glass, filtration, foundry, or otherwise scarce specialty grades; the company proxy may understate retention for those niches.
q.rationale: Frac sand dominates industry tonnage and is exposed to spot pricing, concentrated buyers, basin-local alternatives, idle capacity, and logistics competition. Long-term contracts, specification-qualified reserves, rail access, and diversified specialty products can preserve savings, but over five years a material share should pass through in price or service terms.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level gross-margin bridges after productivity projects, take-or-pay and spot contract terms, bid and renewal histories, delivered-cost curves, capacity utilization, and price realization by frac versus specialty product would materially change retention.
caveats: Smart Sand's mix is more heavily Northern White frac sand and logistics than the full NAICS population.
The evidence observes market and contract structure, not AI-benefit retention directly.

d5.bridge: USGS directly measures industrial sand but offers recent actuals and qualitative drivers rather than a five-year constant-quality forecast: 2025 tonnage fell 5% to 120 million tons, 81% served hydraulic fracturing and related well uses, and oversupply idled production. Smart Sand expects moderate frac growth from longer laterals, rising sand intensity, LNG, and power demand and describes specialty demand as stable, but it is one supplier with a particular reserve and logistics position. I combine the national contraction with those opposing drivers into a flat central five-year ratio and wide tails; management optimism may overstate demand, while USGS's one-year decline may understate a cyclical recovery.
d5.rationale: The basket is dominated by a cyclical oilfield input. Lower rig counts, regional wet or dry sand, oversupply, alternative proppants, and capital discipline weigh against volume, while more sand per completed well, longer laterals, potential natural-gas drilling, and stable glass, foundry, filtration, and other industrial uses provide support. No directional five-year call is sufficiently strong to move the base away from 1.0.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price and constant-quality forecast split by frac basin, glass, foundry, filtration, building products, and other uses, combined with rig completions, proppant intensity, announced capacity, and LMM contract volumes, would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Frac sand represented approximately 81% of 2025 tonnage, so oil-and-gas cycles dominate the national total.
Specialty industrial sand has different demand and pricing dynamics that the aggregate range obscures.
Company outlooks are management judgments and can reflect product quality, geography, and share gains rather than industry demand.

o.bridge: USGS observes current substitution from in-basin wet or dry sand and increased petroleum-coke lightweight proppant use, while Smart Sand discusses regional sand and alternative proppants; these sources do not measure the five-year share of the current basket requiring an operator under the lens. I interpret them as modest displacement risk around a high physical-operator requirement, because even substituted industrial sand must be mined and processed, whereas manufactured proppants or eliminated fracturing stages can remove lens demand. The bridge may overstate displacement if alternatives remain niche.
o.rationale: Industrial sand remains a physical, specification-sensitive input that must be extracted, washed, screened, dried or otherwise prepared, tested, and delivered by an accountable operator. Software cannot self-serve it away. Alternative proppants, in-basin sourcing, customer-owned capacity, or production methods that reduce frac sand can displace part of the current operator basket.
o.change_evidence: Five-year proppant-technology forecasts, customer make-versus-buy surveys, captive-capacity announcements, product qualification data, and substitution rates by glass, foundry, filtration, and oilfield use would materially change the operator-required share.
caveats: Regional or wet sand may displace a specific incumbent while still requiring another industrial-sand operator.
The estimate concerns operator necessity, not whether current independent LMM operators retain share.

narrative.executive_view: Industrial sand mining has a credible AI and automation opportunity in mobile equipment, process plants, laboratories, and logistics, but its commercial profile is dominated by volatile frac sand. USGS reports falling 2025 quantity, oversupply, and 81% oilfield-related tonnage. Specialty glass, foundry, filtration, and other grades diversify demand and can improve retention, but the frozen acquisition pool is only 44 estimated firms and must be screened plant by plant for operating status, end-market mix, permits, reserve quality, and customer concentration.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can support autonomous or assisted loading and hauling, dispatch, conveyor and dryer control, predictive maintenance, vision inspection, laboratory-data review, reserve modeling, railcar planning, quoting, and safety monitoring. NIOSH documents growing mining adoption and the need for safety research around mobile autonomy. Physical excavation, plant maintenance, sampling, silica controls, rail loading, and accountable quality release remain operator-intensive; mixed fleets, connectivity, validation, and weak-cycle capital budgets restrain implementation.
narrative.value_capture: Value capture is weakest in oversupplied frac-sand markets where concentrated customers use spot or short contracts and compare regional delivered cost. Take-or-pay agreements, scarce reserve characteristics, reliable rail or terminal access, and specification-qualified specialty sand can protect value, but contracts may also lock in prices. A buyer should underwrite retention separately for each product and basin rather than use a blended corporate margin.
narrative.firm_availability: USGS reports 131 companies and 207 operations in 2025, while the frozen LMM estimate is 44 firms. The sector is small enough that idled plants, captive operations, strategic-scale firms, and multi-mine ownership can materially change the count. Disclosures describe consolidation, closures, bankruptcies, and asset sales, which create transaction events but often not transferable going concerns; broad succession data is only an anchor.
narrative.demand_durability: USGS estimates 120 million tons sold or used in 2025, down 5%, with apparent consumption down 6%; oversupply caused operations to reduce or idle production. Longer laterals and higher proppant intensity can offset fewer rigs, and natural-gas demand plus LNG could support completions. Glass, foundry, filtration, building-products, and other industrial uses are steadier but much smaller. The most honest five-year base is flat with wide downside and upside cases.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are oil and gas prices and completion activity, oversupply, in-basin and wet-sand substitution, alternative proppants, customer concentration, rail dependence, take-or-pay obligations, energy-intensive drying, crystalline-silica rules, zoning and permits, reclamation, reserve quality, weather, and idle capacity. The frozen compensation ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and the firm count is margin-bridged; evidence for LMM eligibility, transactions, implemented AI, and retained savings is sparse.
narrative.principal_driver: Automation can improve equipment, process, laboratory, and logistics productivity, while longer wells and stable specialty uses preserve substantial physical sand demand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Frac sand dominates tonnage, exposing the industry to oversupply, concentrated buyers, spot pricing, regional substitution, and oilfield cycles that can overwhelm operating improvements.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: Official scope: NAICS 212322 covers operating industrial-grade sand pits, dredging, washing, screening, and preparation, while construction-grade sand is classified separately.

sources.title: Industries at a Glance: Mining (except Oil and Gas): NAICS 212
sources.supports: Broad mining occupation proxy: 20,900 excavating/loading operators, 18,530 operating engineers/equipment operators, 12,810 heavy truck drivers, and 9,080 extraction supervisors in 2025.

sources.title: NIOSH Automation and Emerging Technologies Partnership
sources.supports: Mining automation evidence covering autonomous mobile equipment, collision avoidance, wireless communications, digital sensors, human-machine interfaces, data analytics, implementation research, and safety constraints.

sources.title: USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries 2026: Sand and Gravel (Industrial)
sources.supports: Industry structure and demand: 120 million tons and $4.5 billion estimated in 2025, quantity down 5%, 131 companies and 207 operations, 81% of tonnage for frac/well uses, oversupply-driven idling, lower rig counts, and growing in-basin and petroleum-coke proppant substitution.

sources.title: Smart Sand, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Contract, competition, and demand proxy: spot, short-term, and take-or-pay sales; highly competitive proppant markets; two customers at 39% of 2025 revenue; moderate 2025 demand decline; stable specialty-industrial demand; and longer laterals, sand intensity, LNG, and power demand as potential growth drivers.

sources.title: Mammoth Energy Services, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Operational and availability proxy: Northern White frac-sand mining uses open pits, trucks or conveyors, wet processing, drying, screening, and silos; disclosed plants included idled and sold facilities and substantial unused permitted capacity.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Broad succession proxy: 22% of employer-business owners report a planned sale or ownership transfer within five years, and 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older.
